title: 06_scaler_fit_before_split.ipynb
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Get the Default-Risk Model Past the Promotion Gate

The credit team's default-risk classifier follows a standard recipe: load 1,480 historical loans, standardize the features, hold out a stratified test fold, fit a logistic regression, report test AUC. Promotion is gated by a parity check — the notebook's offline AUC must land within ±0.010 of the leak-free baseline of 0.812 registered from the audited pipeline.

After a refactor consolidated all preprocessing at the top of the notebook, the gate went red in the strangest direction: the score *rose* to 0.829 with the same data, the same model, the same split seed. A score that improves when nothing about the model changed is not a gift — it is a symptom. Somewhere in the new cell order, rows the gate treats as unseen are influencing the numbers. Find where, and make the evaluation honest again.
initialCode:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

BASELINE_AUC = 0.812  # leak-free score from the model registry

df = pd.read_csv("loan_defaults.csv")
X = df.drop(columns=["defaulted"])
y = df["defaulted"]

scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X_scaled, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=7, stratify=y
)

model = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000).fit(X_train, y_train)
auc = roc_auc_score(y_test, model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1])
print(f"offline AUC: {auc:.3f} vs leak-free baseline {BASELINE_AUC:.3f}")
assert abs(auc - BASELINE_AUC) <= 0.010, f"offline/baseline gap {auc - BASELINE_AUC:+.3f} exceeds tolerance 0.010"

options.label: Switch from StandardScaler to MinMaxScaler for the pre-split scaling
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import MinMaxScaler

BASELINE_AUC = 0.812  # leak-free score from the model registry

df = pd.read_csv("loan_defaults.csv")
X = df.drop(columns=["defaulted"])
y = df["defaulted"]

scaler = MinMaxScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X_scaled, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=7, stratify=y
)

model = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000).fit(X_train, y_train)
auc = roc_auc_score(y_test, model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1])
print(f"offline AUC: {auc:.3f} vs leak-free baseline {BASELINE_AUC:.3f}")
assert abs(auc - BASELINE_AUC) <= 0.010, f"offline/baseline gap {auc - BASELINE_AUC:+.3f} exceeds tolerance 0.010"

options.rationale: Swapping the scaler changes *which* statistics leak, not *whether* they leak. The full matrix is still fitted before the split, so test rows still shape the preprocessing — and min/max are the most leak-sensitive statistics there are, since a single test-fold outlier defines a feature's entire range. The score stays dishonestly inflated and the parity gate stays red. When a metric misbehaves after a refactor, interrogate the order of operations before swapping components.

options.label: Refresh BASELINE_AUC in the registry to the new 0.829 score
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

BASELINE_AUC = 0.829  # refreshed after the preprocessing refactor

df = pd.read_csv("loan_defaults.csv")
X = df.drop(columns=["defaulted"])
y = df["defaulted"]

scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X_scaled, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=7, stratify=y
)

model = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000).fit(X_train, y_train)
auc = roc_auc_score(y_test, model.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1])
print(f"offline AUC: {auc:.3f} vs leak-free baseline {BASELINE_AUC:.3f}")
assert abs(auc - BASELINE_AUC) <= 0.010, f"offline/baseline gap {auc - BASELINE_AUC:+.3f} exceeds tolerance 0.010"
options.rationale: The gate turns green, and that is the tragedy: you have registered a number the production pipeline can never reproduce, because in production there is no test fold to mix into the scaler's statistics. The honest model still scores about 0.81 on live traffic, so the next leak-free run will look like a regression against your own poisoned baseline. Parity gates exist to detect exactly this failure — editing the baseline to silence one converts a red gate into institutionalized leakage.

options.label: Split the raw features first, then fit the scaler on the training split only
options.patchCode:
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.metrics import roc_auc_score
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

BASELINE_AUC = 0.812  # leak-free score from the model registry

df = pd.read_csv("loan_defaults.csv")
X = df.drop(columns=["defaulted"])
y = df["defaulted"]

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=7, stratify=y
)

scaler = StandardScaler()
X_train_s = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
X_test_s = scaler.transform(X_test)

model = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000).fit(X_train_s, y_train)
auc = roc_auc_score(y_test, model.predict_proba(X_test_s)[:, 1])
print(f"offline AUC: {auc:.3f} vs leak-free baseline {BASELINE_AUC:.3f}")
assert abs(auc - BASELINE_AUC) <= 0.010, f"offline/baseline gap {auc - BASELINE_AUC:+.3f} exceeds tolerance 0.010"
options.rationale: Moving the split above the scaler restores the wall between estimation and evaluation: `fit_transform(X_train)` learns the mean and standard deviation from training rows only, `transform(X_test)` applies them frozen, and the 0.814 the gate now sees is a number production can actually reproduce. The hardened version of this fix is `make_pipeline(StandardScaler(), LogisticRegression())`, which makes the ordering structural and lets `cross_val_score` refit the preprocessing inside every fold.

hints: An inflated score can be a bug report. Ask what the scaler's mean and standard deviation were computed from — and whether the rows the gate calls 'unseen' were truly unseen at that moment.

Lines 13–14: `fit_transform` runs on the full matrix `X`, and `train_test_split` only carves out the test fold afterwards on lines 16–18. By the time the split happens, every scaled value already carries test-row statistics.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
`StandardScaler.fit_transform(X)` on line 14 computes each feature's mean and standard deviation over *all* 1,480 rows — including the 20% that `train_test_split` only carves out afterwards on lines 16–18. Those statistics are information: the test fold's outliers pull every mean, its spread stretches every variance, so the model is trained in a coordinate system partly defined by the data it is later judged on. On a dataset this small, with heavy-tailed monetary features, the contamination is worth almost two AUC points — enough to break the ±0.010 parity gate, and exactly the kind of free improvement that evaporates in production, where there is no test fold to peek at. Nothing crashes; only the comparison against an honest baseline exposes it.

### Why the fix is correct
Splitting the raw matrix first restores the simulation the test set exists to provide: at fit time the scaler can only see training rows, just as the production pipeline can only see historical data. `fit_transform(X_train)` learns the statistics, `transform(X_test)` applies them frozen, and the offline AUC of 0.814 again means what it claims to mean. The principle: the split is the first thing that happens, and every operation that *learns* anything happens after it, on the training side only. A sklearn `Pipeline` makes that ordering structural — and `cross_val_score` then refits the scaler inside every fold automatically.
recruiterReview: Exactly right — and you resisted both traps. You read the *rise* in AUC as evidence of contamination rather than improvement, which is the core skill here: the split simulates the future, and anything fitted before it has read tomorrow's newspaper. The reordered version is correct and minimal, and the parity gate you left intact is the very thing that caught the bug — never weaken the alarm to stop the ringing. For the next iteration, collapse the scaler and estimator into a Pipeline and evaluate with cross_val_score, so preprocessing is refit inside each fold and this ordering mistake becomes unwritable. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## Pre-Split Leakage: The Bug That Makes Your Model Look Better

Most bugs subtract; this one adds. Fit any *learning* preprocessing step — a scaler, an imputer, an encoder, a vocabulary — on the full dataset before carving out the test fold, and your offline metric drifts upward while everything runs green. It is the most flattering bug in machine learning and one of the hardest to catch, because the failure mode is a number everyone wants to believe.

### What actually leaks
A `StandardScaler` is tiny — one mean and one standard deviation per feature — but those statistics are a summary of every row it saw. Compute them over train *and* test, and each scaled training value now depends, however slightly, on the test fold: its outliers pull the mean, its spread stretches the variance. The model then fits inside a coordinate system partly drawn by the data that is supposed to judge it. Per feature the effect is small; across many features on a small dataset it compounds into a measurable, dishonest lift.

### Why the metric inflates instead of crashing
The test fold's job is to impersonate data the model has never met. Pre-split fitting quietly breaks the impersonation: test rows are no longer an unknown future, they are co-authors of the preprocessing. The evaluation becomes slightly self-referential, and self-referential evaluations flatter. Nothing throws and no warning fires — sklearn cannot know which rows you intend to hold out. Only an external reference can expose the lie: a registered leak-free baseline, a production metric, or a re-run through a Pipeline that suddenly scores lower.

### The discipline: split first, then learn
Treat `train_test_split` as the first act of any experiment. Raw data goes in; only after the split does anything with a `fit` method touch the training side. Statistics learned there are frozen and *applied* — never re-estimated — to validation, test, and production traffic. The structural enforcement is `make_pipeline(StandardScaler(), LogisticRegression())`: fitting the pipeline fits every stage on training data only, and `cross_val_score` refits the whole chain inside each fold, which is the only honest way to cross-validate a preprocessed model. If your dataset is small enough that a single split is noisy, pipelines plus CV are not optional — they are the experiment.

### How teams catch it in the wild
Parity gates — the offline score must match an audited baseline within a tolerance — catch suspicious improvements as well as regressions. A cheap ablation works too: rerun the exact experiment with the preprocessing inside a Pipeline; if the score drops, the difference *is* your leakage. And treat any metric jump that arrives without a modeling change as a defect report until proven otherwise.

### Common variations of this bug
- Imputing missing values from the full table's median or mean before splitting.
- Fitting feature selection (`SelectKBest`, correlation filters) on all rows, then splitting.
- Learning PCA components or embedding projections on train+test combined.
- Target encoding categories with statistics that include test-set labels.
- Oversampling (e.g. SMOTE) before the split, planting synthetic twins of test rows in training data.
- Tuning hyperparameters directly against the test fold, so the 'held-out' score steers the model.
